narrative:
I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates an 89% chance you'll struggle to pass the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile shows strong commitment as a full-time student and a middle-income family background, but it highlights some key academic hurdles compared to typical applicants. You ranked in the bottom decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, far below the average student's middle-of-the-pack performance. Your LSAT score of 22 is also notably lower than the typical 37, and your undergraduate GPA of 2.5 trails the average of 3.2. As an Asian male, you're in a less common demographic group among applicants, where most are White.

The model's decision was driven primarily by your third-year school decile, which had the strongest pull toward predicting bar failure because it signals consistently low performance relative to peers. Next in influence was your LSAT score, which significantly nudged the prediction toward failure due to its below-average mark. Your first-year decile followed closely, reinforcing concerns about early academic struggles. Your racial background then played a role in tipping the scales toward failure, as it deviated from the dominant White majority in the data. Family income came after that, with your middle bracket not providing the boost seen in higher earners on average.

Your undergraduate GPA added further weight toward the failure prediction, while your choice to study full-time had minimal impact. Interestingly, being male slightly favored a passing prediction, bucking the trend just a bit. Overall, these academic metrics—especially your school deciles and LSAT—outweighed everything else in the model's eyes.

To improve your odds next time, focus on boosting metrics you can influence, like retaking the LSAT for a higher score or pursuing graduate courses to lift your GPA equivalent. Building a stronger academic track record through targeted study habits could help mimic the profiles of students who pass the bar. With dedication, you can shift these key factors and reapply stronger. Keep pushing—you've got the full-time drive to make it happen.
